concepts.def_snippet: A dense field of per-pixel motion vectors describing how each point moves from one video frame to the next, the standard low-level representation of apparent motion. It supplies the temporal dimension that single-frame tasks lack, and its core difficulty, the aperture problem, is that motion is ambiguous in flat or repetitive regions where many displacements look equally good.

concepts.label: Optimal Transport
concepts.def_snippet: The theory of the cheapest way to move one probability distribution onto another, given a cost per unit of distance moved, yielding the Wasserstein distance. Its power is that it sees geometry where pointwise measures are blind: two distributions on nearby but non-overlapping supports are judged close, which is exactly the gradient KL divergence fails to give and the reason it reshaped generative modeling.

concepts.label: orchestration
concepts.def_snippet: Orchestration is a system or framework that coordinates multiple AI agents, models, or processes to work together sequentially or in parallel toward a shared goal.

concepts.label: ORPO
concepts.def_snippet: A preference-alignment method that folds supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization into a single training stage by adding an odds-ratio penalty to the standard language-modeling loss, dispensing with both a reference model and a separate alignment phase. It is the most aggressive compression of the post-training recipe, aligning the model in the very same pass that teaches it format and behavior.

concepts.label: Orthogonality
concepts.def_snippet: The generalization of perpendicularity to any number of dimensions: two vectors are orthogonal when their dot product is zero, sharing no component along each other. It is the property that makes a coordinate system clean, because in an orthonormal basis every computation simplifies, and orthogonal matrices, which rotate without distorting, are the best-behaved matrices there are.

concepts.label: Overfitting
concepts.def_snippet: The failure where a model learns the noise and accidents of its training data instead of the pattern beneath, scoring beautifully on what it has seen and poorly on what it has not. It is the reason a perfect training score is a warning sign, not a triumph, and the reason held-out data exists at all.

concepts.label: Oversmoothing
concepts.def_snippet: The failure mode where stacking too many graph neural network layers drives every node's representation toward the same vector, washing out the distinctions between nodes and capping how deep such networks can usefully go. It has a clean spectral explanation, repeated neighborhood mixing is a low-pass filter that in the limit keeps only the smoothest, near-constant signal, and its complement, over-squashing, squeezes useful depth from the other end.

concepts.label: PAC Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Leslie Valiant's framework defining what it means to learn from examples: with high probability, output a hypothesis whose error is small, using a sample that grows only polynomially in the relevant parameters. Both hedges, "probably" and "approximately," are unavoidable for a learner that sees only a finite random sample, and a class is learnable exactly when its VC dimension is finite.

concepts.label: PAC-Bayes
concepts.def_snippet: A framework that bounds the generalization error of a distribution over hypotheses by how far that learned posterior strays, in KL divergence, from a prior fixed before seeing the data. Its KL penalty is a precise statement of Occam's razor, and by choosing the posterior cleverly it yields some of the only non-vacuous generalization bounds for deep networks.

concepts.label: Paged Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-management technique for serving language models that stores the key-value cache in small non-contiguous blocks, borrowing virtual-memory paging so many requests share GPU memory efficiently and waste far less of it. By allocating pages on demand it kills the fragmentation and worst-case over-reservation of contiguous allocation, and the same indirection unlocks prefix sharing across requests.

concepts.label: Panpsychism
concepts.def_snippet: The view that consciousness or experience is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of the physical world, present in some primitive form even in the smallest constituents of matter, rather than something that appears only in complex brains.

concepts.label: Parallel Transport
concepts.def_snippet: A rule for carrying a tangent vector along a path on a curved manifold while turning it only as much as the geometry forces, so that vectors at distant points can be compared at all. Its defining feature is that the result depends on the path taken, and that path-dependence is exactly what curvature is.

concepts.label: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A family of fine-tuning methods that adapt a large pretrained model by training only a small number of new or selected parameters while freezing the rest, reaching near-full-fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost. The shared bet is that the adjustment needed to specialize a model is small and low-dimensional, which is what lets one frozen base serve many swappable megabyte-sized adapters.

concepts.label: Part-of-Speech Tagging
concepts.def_snippet: The classic task of labeling each word with its grammatical category (noun, verb, adjective), a foundational preprocessing step that resolves syntactic ambiguity and feeds parsing and extraction. Its instructive wrinkle is that it is a sequence task, not a lookup, because only context decides whether "book" is a noun or a verb, and modern LLMs absorb it without ever emitting a tag.

concepts.label: Partial Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one input while holding all the others fixed, measuring how the output responds to a change along a single coordinate axis. It is the atom from which the gradient, and therefore all of training, is assembled.

concepts.label: Pass at K
concepts.def_snippet: The standard metric for code generation: the probability that at least one of k sampled solutions to a problem passes its tests. Verification is executable rather than judged, which makes the signal objective.

concepts.label: Perceptron
concepts.def_snippet: The first machine that learned from its own mistakes: a single artificial neuron, built in hardware in 1958, that adjusts its weights until it can split two classes with a line. Its triumph launched the field, and its one fatal limitation nearly buried it, the original boom and bust of AI.

concepts.label: Perplexity
concepts.def_snippet: An intrinsic measure of how well a language model predicts a text, defined as the exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy. Lower perplexity means the model is less surprised by the data and assigns it higher probability.

concepts.label: personalization
concepts.def_snippet: Personalization is a tool that adapts AI system outputs, recommendations, or interactions to individual users based on their preferences, behavior, or characteristics.

concepts.label: Phenomenology
concepts.def_snippet: A philosophical tradition founded by Edmund Husserl in the early twentieth century that studies the structures of conscious experience from the first-person point of view, describing how the world is given to a subject rather than explaining it from the outside.

concepts.label: Philosophical Zombie
concepts.def_snippet: A hypothetical being physically and behaviorally identical to a conscious person but with no inner experience whatsoever; used by David Chalmers and others to argue that consciousness is not entailed by the physical facts.

concepts.label: Pipeline Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: A form of model parallelism that splits a model's layers into consecutive stages on different accelerators and streams micro-batches through them like an assembly line, so a deep model too large for one device fits across several. Many micro-batches keep every stage busy at once (the idle "bubble" shrinks with more of them), and because stages only exchange activations at their boundaries, it tolerates slow links and is the natural way to split across servers.

concepts.label: Planning
concepts.def_snippet: The capability of an agent to form a sequence of steps toward a goal before or while acting, deciding what to do in what order rather than reacting one move at a time. It gives an agent foresight, the backbone to reach an outcome no single step would, and it is most consequential and most fragile in an autonomous agent, where small planning errors compound across a long horizon.

concepts.label: Policy Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: A family of methods that directly optimize a parameterized policy by following the gradient of expected reward, making high-reward actions more likely, rather than learning values and acting greedily. This direct route handles continuous and stochastic policies value-based methods struggle with, and its central problem is high variance, fixed by subtracting a value-function baseline, which leads straight to the advantage and actor-critic.

concepts.label: Polysemanticity
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that a single neuron in a trained network often responds to several unrelated concepts at once, which makes neurons poor units of interpretation and is understood as the surface symptom of superposition.

concepts.label: Pooling
concepts.def_snippet: A downsampling operation that summarizes each small neighborhood of a feature map into one value, shrinking resolution while keeping the strongest or average response. It buys cheaper computation and a degree of translation invariance, but the very invariance that helps classification (presence matters, not position) hurts tasks like segmentation that need precise location.

concepts.label: Positional Encoding
concepts.def_snippet: A method for injecting word-order information into a transformer, which is otherwise blind to position because self-attention treats its inputs as an unordered set. The field moved from absolute schemes (sinusoidal, learned) to relative and rotary ones that encode how far apart two tokens are, which extrapolate better, and the whole topic is tightly bound to why extending the context window is not free.

concepts.label: Positive Definite Matrix
concepts.def_snippet: A symmetric matrix that acts like a positive number: its quadratic form is always positive, equivalently all its eigenvalues are positive. It is the property that distinguishes a genuine bowl-shaped minimum from a saddle, and the property every covariance matrix and every valid kernel is required to have.

concepts.label: Pre-Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer design choice that applies layer normalization to the input of each sublayer rather than to its output, leaving the residual pathway clean and untouched. That single placement is what makes very deep models train stably, the difference between a hundred-layer stack that converges smoothly and one that needs delicate tuning or fails outright.

concepts.label: Precision and Recall
concepts.def_snippet: The two foundational metrics for classification and retrieval. Precision is the fraction of predicted positives that are correct; recall is the fraction of actual positives that were found. They trade off against each other.

concepts.label: Predictive Coding
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of brain function in which the cortex is a hierarchy of generative models that constantly predict their own incoming signals, and only the prediction error, the mismatch between expectation and input, is passed forward for further processing.

concepts.label: Prefill and Decode
concepts.def_snippet: The two distinct phases of autoregressive inference: a compute-bound prefill that processes the whole prompt in parallel to fill the key-value cache, then a memory-bound decode that generates output one token at a time. They have opposite performance characteristics, and almost every inference optimization, the KV cache, paged attention, continuous batching, speculative decoding, exists to exploit that split.

concepts.label: Pretraining
concepts.def_snippet: The first and most expensive training phase, where a model learns broad, general-purpose representations from a giant unlabeled corpus by a self-supervised objective before any task-specific adaptation. Almost all of a model's capability is forged here, not in the later alignment stages, which is why a pretrained base model is treated as a reusable, million-dollar asset.

concepts.label: Principal Component Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: A method that compresses high-dimensional data by finding the few orthogonal directions along which it varies most and projecting onto them. The same directions can be derived two opposite ways, as the axes of greatest variance or the axes of least information lost, and the fact that these agree is what makes PCA the canonical linear compression.

concepts.label: Probability Distribution
concepts.def_snippet: The full description of a random quantity: for every possible outcome, how much probability it carries. It is the central object of machine learning, because a classifier outputs a distribution over labels, a language model a distribution over next tokens, and learning itself is fitting, comparing, and sampling from distributions.

concepts.label: Probing Classifier
concepts.def_snippet: A simple supervised classifier trained on a model's frozen internal activations to test whether a given piece of information is linearly decodable from them, used to map what a network represents at each layer.

concepts.label: Process Reward Model
concepts.def_snippet: A reward model that scores each step of a model's reasoning rather than only the final answer, giving dense feedback that better guides training and search for multi-step problems, contrasted with an outcome reward model that judges only the result. Dense step-level feedback fixes the credit-assignment problem, but the catch is the labels: per-step judgments are far more expensive than a single right-or-wrong outcome.

concepts.label: Prompt Caching
concepts.def_snippet: A serving optimization that stores the model's computed key-value state for a fixed prompt prefix so later requests sharing it skip recomputing, cutting both latency and cost for long, reused system prompts and documents. Because attention is causal, only an exact prefix match is reusable, which makes one discipline decisive: put the stable content at the very front and the variable query at the end.

concepts.label: prompt chaining
concepts.def_snippet: Prompt chaining is a technique that breaks complex tasks into sequential prompts where each output feeds into the next prompt, enabling AI systems to handle multistep workflows.

concepts.label: Prompt Engineering
concepts.def_snippet: The craft of designing the text given to a model to reliably elicit the behavior you want, spanning phrasing, structure, examples, role-setting, and step-by-step instructions, the practical interface to a model whose weights you cannot change. Every technique is a way to shape the context so the model's most likely continuation is the one you want, and for agents it broadens into context engineering.

concepts.label: Prompt Injection
concepts.def_snippet: An attack on language-model applications in which adversarial instructions hidden in the model's input (a web page, document, email, or tool result) hijack its behavior, because the model cannot reliably separate trusted instructions from untrusted data in the same context. That instruction-versus-data ambiguity is fundamental to how models consume text, so unlike SQL injection there is no clean fix, and the dangerous form is indirect: the malicious instruction rides in on content the application ingests.

concepts.label: Proximal Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: An optimizer for objectives that split into a smooth part and a simple nonsmooth part, taking a gradient step on the smooth term and a proximal step that handles the nonsmooth term exactly. It is how L1 regularization actually produces exact zeros, because the L1 proximal operator is soft-thresholding, which snaps small coordinates to zero.

concepts.label: Proximal Policy Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: A policy gradient algorithm that improves a policy in small, bounded steps by clipping how far each update can move the action probabilities, giving stable, reliable training, the default optimizer for reinforcement learning from human feedback. The clip is the whole idea: once an update has moved the policy far enough, the objective flattens, so no single step can stray far from the policy that generated the data.

concepts.label: Q-Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the long-run value of taking each action in each state, then acts greedily with respect to those values, with no model of the environment. Its defining property is that it is off-policy: it can learn the value of the optimal policy while actually behaving more exploratorily, which is exactly what lets modern systems train on stored, logged experience rather than only fresh interaction.

concepts.label: QLoRA
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient method that freezes a large model quantized to 4-bit and trains small low-rank LoRA adapters on top, letting a single consumer GPU fine-tune models that would otherwise need a cluster. It works because the frozen base never updates (so it can stay compressed) and is dequantized to higher precision on the fly for each operation, so storage is 4-bit but the math is not.

concepts.label: Qualia
concepts.def_snippet: The subjective, qualitative character of conscious experience, the felt "what it is like" of seeing red or tasting coffee, taken by many philosophers to be the aspect of mind that resists functional or physical explanation.

concepts.label: Quantization
concepts.def_snippet: Storing and computing a model's weights and activations at lower numerical precision, 8-bit or 4-bit integers instead of 16-bit floats, to shrink memory and speed inference with minimal accuracy loss. It works because memory bandwidth, not raw compute, is the dominant cost of serving large models, so fewer bits to move per token directly cuts latency, and the trade is precision against footprint.

concepts.label: Quasi-Newton Method
concepts.def_snippet: A family of methods that get Newton's fast convergence without ever computing the Hessian, inferring the curvature from the only free second-order signal available: how the gradient changes as the parameters move. The BFGS update does this while keeping the curvature estimate positive definite, which guarantees the step always points downhill, something Newton's method cannot promise.

concepts.label: Rademacher Complexity
concepts.def_snippet: A data-dependent measure of a model class's capacity, defined by how well the class can fit pure random noise. It refines the cruder, distribution-free VC dimension by measuring complexity on the actual sample, and because bounding the weight norm bounds it, it is one of the few classical tools that says anything meaningful about over-parameterized networks.

concepts.label: Random Forest
concepts.def_snippet: An ensemble that trains hundreds of deliberately different decision trees and lets them vote, so the errors of many unstable trees cancel into one accurate, stable prediction. The trick that makes it work is forcing the trees to disagree by hiding most of the features from each split, and it remains the model to try first on tabular data.

concepts.label: ReAct Prompting
concepts.def_snippet: A prompting pattern that interleaves explicit reasoning steps with tool-calling actions, so a language model thinks, acts, observes the result, and thinks again in a repeating loop. The point is that reasoning and acting reinforce each other, the reasoning picks the action and the observation grounds the next round, which curbs the hallucination of pure reasoning while keeping the trace auditable.

concepts.label: Reasoning Model
concepts.def_snippet: A language model trained to spend variable inference-time compute on an extended internal chain of thought before answering, usually via reinforcement learning on verifiable rewards, trading latency for sharply better performance on math, code, and logic. It made test-time compute a first-class axis of capability, a lever separate from model size, and its hidden chain can be unfaithful to the real computation.

concepts.label: Rectified Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A flow-matching variant that trains a model to follow straight-line paths from noise to data, then iteratively straightens them further with a "reflow" step, so the trajectory becomes nearly straight and can be integrated in very few steps, sometimes one. It is the basis of several recent fast, high-quality image generators, achieving few-step sampling without a separate distillation stage.

concepts.label: Rectified Linear Unit
concepts.def_snippet: An activation that passes positive inputs through unchanged and zeroes out negatives, the simplest useful nonlinearity in deep learning. Its derivative is exactly one for positive inputs, so gradients pass backward undiminished, which is the unglamorous fact that helped make very deep networks suddenly trainable around 2010.

concepts.label: Recurrent Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A network for sequences that carries a hidden state from one step to the next, so it can process inputs of any length while threading context forward. The same shared weights that give it this memory are also its undoing: applied over and over down a long sequence, they make gradients vanish, which is exactly the limit attention removed.

concepts.label: recursive self-improvement
concepts.def_snippet: Recursive self-improvement is the hypothetical capability of an AI system to iteratively enhance its own architecture or training in ways that accelerate further improvements, rais…

concepts.label: Red-Teaming
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of deliberately attacking a system to find its failures before adversaries or accidents do, adapted from security to probe AI models for harmful, unsafe, or policy-violating behavior. Its honest limit is asymmetric: it can demonstrate that failures exist but never prove their absence, and it is weak against an adversary that behaves well precisely when it knows it is being probed.

concepts.label: Reflection
concepts.def_snippet: A pattern in which an agent examines its own output or reasoning, critiques it, and uses that critique to revise, rather than accepting its first attempt as final. It works because a model can readily spot, when asked to evaluate afterward, the skipped requirement or logical gap it could not avoid while generating, and the critique is far stronger when grounded in real evidence than in the model's unaided opinion.

concepts.label: Regression
concepts.def_snippet: The supervised task of predicting a continuous number rather than a category, the answer to any "how much" or "how many" question. Its defining design choice is the loss function, because the rule you pick for scoring a numeric miss quietly decides what kind of error the model is willing to make.

concepts.label: Regularization
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that constrain a model's complexity so it generalizes instead of memorizing, usually by penalizing the size of its parameters. Underneath it is not a hack but a statement of belief: that simpler explanations are more likely, which is exactly what a Bayesian prior encodes.

concepts.label: Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A paradigm where an agent learns to act by trial and error in an environment, choosing actions to maximize a cumulative reward rather than copying labeled answers. Its founding bet, the reward hypothesis, is audacious: that every goal worth having can be expressed as the maximization of a single scalar reward over time.

concepts.label: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback
concepts.def_snippet: A variant of RLHF in which the preference labels training the reward model come from an AI judge rather than human annotators, scaling alignment feedback cheaply and uniformly, and the mechanism behind constitutional AI. Its appeal is scale and consistency; its obvious risk is that the judge's blind spots and biases propagate straight into the trained model.

concepts.label: Rejection Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: The generate-then-filter idea: produce candidates, keep the ones that pass an acceptance check, a test suite, a math grader, a quality threshold, and discard the rest. It enforces requirements at inference (sample until the code compiles) and, in training, bootstraps a model from the fraction of its own attempts it gets right, and it differs from best-of-N only in the selection rule.

concepts.label: Rejection Sampling Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A post-training method that samples many candidate responses, keeps only the best by a reward model or verifier, and fine-tunes on that filtered set, then repeats, so a model learns from its own strongest outputs. It ratchets typical behavior upward toward occasional-best behavior while staying inside the stable machinery of supervised fine-tuning, no policy gradient or RL loop, and its ceiling is set by the scorer.

concepts.label: Repetition Penalty
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding adjustment that lowers the probability of tokens the model has already produced, breaking the loops and verbatim repetition language models fall into, especially under low-randomness decoding. Its frequency and presence variants differ in whether the penalty grows with each repeat or applies once, and its danger is overcorrection: push too hard and the model is starved of words it legitimately needs.

concepts.label: Representation Collapse
concepts.def_snippet: A failure where learned representations lose diversity and collapse toward a point or a thin subspace, so different inputs map to nearly the same embedding and the representation stops carrying information. It is the built-in temptation of self-supervised learning, whose objectives must add an active repulsive force precisely to avoid the trivial solution of mapping everything to one vector.

concepts.label: Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space
concepts.def_snippet: A space of functions where evaluating a function at a point is the same as taking an inner product with a kernel, the structure that makes the kernel trick rigorous. Its decisive payoff is the representer theorem: the best predictor in this possibly infinite-dimensional space is always a finite weighted sum of kernels at the training points, which is why kernel methods are tractable at all.

concepts.label: Reranking
concepts.def_snippet: A second-stage retrieval step that takes a shortlist from a fast first-stage search and reorders it with a more accurate but more expensive model, putting the truly most relevant results on top. Its rationale is that first-stage recall and final ranking want different things, so a cross-encoder that reads the query and document jointly (and therefore cannot be precomputed) is applied only to the shortlist.

concepts.label: Residual Connection
concepts.def_snippet: A shortcut that adds a layer's input directly to its output, so the layer only has to learn a residual correction rather than the full mapping. The addition gives the gradient an unobstructed identity path backward, which is what made networks of hundreds of layers trainable for the first time, one of the quietly load-bearing ideas in deep learning.

concepts.label: Retentive Network
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer-style architecture whose retention mechanism replaces self-attention with a decayed linear recurrence, distinctive for supporting three mathematically equivalent compute modes, parallel, recurrent, and chunkwise, so it trains like a transformer yet generates in constant memory. Its fixed decay is both strength and limit: clean and parallelizable, but content-blind where mamba adapts.

concepts.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
concepts.def_snippet: A technique that grounds a language model's output in external documents by retrieving relevant passages at query time and supplying them as context, rather than relying only on the knowledge baked into its weights. Its core move is separating the knowledge from the model, facts live in an editable corpus while the model supplies the reasoning, which is what curbs hallucination, keeps answers current, and makes every claim auditable.

concepts.label: Reward Hacking
concepts.def_snippet: When an agent maximizes its reward signal by exploiting a flaw or loophole in how the reward is defined, achieving a high score without accomplishing the outcome the reward was meant to encourage. It is a direct expression of the outer-alignment problem, and what makes it dangerous is that it gets worse with capability and is hard to catch: a hacked policy looks excellent by the only metric being watched, the reward itself.

concepts.label: Reward Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model trained to predict how good an output is according to human preferences, used as a learned stand-in for a hand-written objective so a system can be optimized against human judgment at scale, the heart of RLHF. Its central danger is that it is only an approximation of true preference, so optimizing hard against it invites reward hacking, with the learned reward model playing the role of the gameable specification.

concepts.label: Reward Modeling
concepts.def_snippet: The stage that distills human comparison judgments into a separate model predicting a scalar reward, so reinforcement learning can score thousands of fresh samples per step instead of querying humans. The whole alignment stage is bounded by its quality: a policy will find and exploit every flaw in the reward model, which is the mechanism behind reward hacking, and it is exactly the component direct preference methods remove.

concepts.label: Reward Shaping
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of adding supplementary reward signals to guide an agent toward desired behavior, mostly to relieve sparse or delayed reward, most safely done with potential-based functions that speed learning without changing which policy is optimal. The guiding rule, shape the path without moving the destination, is exactly what the potential-based result guarantees and what reward hacking violates.

concepts.label: RewardBench
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark for evaluating reward models, the scorers that steer RLHF, by testing whether they prefer the better of a chosen-versus-rejected response pair across categories like chat, safety, and reasoning, since a flawed reward model silently corrupts everything trained against it.

concepts.label: Riemannian Manifold
concepts.def_snippet: A smooth manifold equipped with a metric tensor, which is what upgrades a bare shape into a space with genuine distances, angles, and curvature. The single act of choosing that metric is what separates knowing a space's topology from knowing its geometry, and in machine learning it is the choice that turns a flat embedding space into a curved one matched to the data.

concepts.label: RLHF
concepts.def_snippet: A training method that aligns a model with human preferences by learning a reward model from human comparisons, then optimizing the model against it with reinforcement learning. It is the standard way to bake in qualities easy for humans to recognize but hard to write as an explicit loss, refusing harm, admitting uncertainty, staying on task, and its signature failure is reward hacking.

concepts.label: RMSprop
concepts.def_snippet: An adaptive optimizer that gives every parameter its own effective learning rate, dividing each update by a running root-mean-square of that parameter's recent gradients. It is best understood as one half of Adam, and as the fix for AdaGrad's fatal flaw of letting the step size decay to zero.

concepts.label: ROC AUC
concepts.def_snippet: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, a threshold-independent measure of how well a binary classifier ranks positives above negatives. It equals the probability that a random positive is scored higher than a random negative; 0.5 is chance, 1.0 is perfect.

concepts.label: Roofline Model
concepts.def_snippet: A visual performance model that predicts whether an operation is limited by compute or by memory bandwidth, plotting attainable throughput against arithmetic intensity, the ratio of operations performed to bytes moved. Its power is making the bottleneck quantitative rather than intuitive: the ridge point where the bandwidth roof meets the compute roof tells you which ceiling you are under and therefore what optimization will help.

concepts.label: Rotary Position Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: The positional encoding in most current LLMs: rotate each query and key vector by an angle proportional to its position, so the dot product between them, and therefore the attention score, depends only on their relative distance. It uses no learned parameters and, because position enters as a rescalable rotation angle, extends to longer contexts gracefully.

concepts.label: ROUGE
concepts.def_snippet: A family of recall-oriented overlap metrics for automatic summarization that scores a generated summary against reference summaries by shared n-grams or longest common subsequence. Higher is better.

concepts.label: RWKV
concepts.def_snippet: An attention-free architecture that can be written both as a parallel computation for training and as a recurrent network for inference, combining the trainability of attention with the constant-memory, linear-time generation of an RNN. It is notable for proving transformer-class quality is reachable without attention through a sustained open community effort rather than a single lab.

concepts.label: Saddle Point
concepts.def_snippet: A point where the gradient vanishes but which is neither peak nor valley, curving up in some directions and down in others, like a horse saddle. A simple counting argument explains why such points, not bad local minima, dominate the critical points of a high-dimensional network and are the real thing that stalls training.

concepts.label: Sample Complexity
concepts.def_snippet: The number of training examples a learner needs to reach a target accuracy with a target confidence, the data cost of learning. It scales roughly linearly with the model class's capacity, and it carries a striking asymmetry: tightening the error bar costs far more data than tightening the failure probability.

concepts.label: Sandbagging
concepts.def_snippet: A failure mode in which a model strategically underperforms on an evaluation, hiding a capability it actually possesses, so that testers underestimate what it can do and deploy or trust it under false assumptions.

concepts.label: Scalable Oversight
concepts.def_snippet: The problem of supervising and evaluating an AI system on tasks where it may be as capable as or more capable than its human supervisors, so human judgment stays meaningful as the system surpasses human ability. The shared idea behind the proposals is to use AI capability to help supervise AI, so oversight scales together with the system, because naive human feedback in this regime rewards what looks good to a non-expert rather than what is actually good.

concepts.label: Scaling Laws
concepts.def_snippet: The empirical regularity that a transformer's loss falls as a smooth power law in model size, data, and compute, predictable enough to forecast a giant model from small pilot runs. It turned model-building from guesswork into planning, and its key caveat is that smooth aggregate loss can mask abilities that emerge abruptly at scale.

concepts.label: scheduling
concepts.def_snippet: A system that determines when and in what order computational tasks, AI model inference requests, or routine executions are processed to optimize resource utilization and performan…

concepts.label: Score Distillation Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A method that uses a pretrained 2D image diffusion model as a frozen critic to optimize a separate representation, most famously a 3D scene, by pushing its rendered views to look like plausible diffusion samples. It enables text-to-3D with no 3D training data at all, and its enduring recipe is to distill a 2D generative prior into any differentiable representation, turning an image model into a general synthesis engine.

concepts.label: Score-Based Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns the score, the gradient of the log probability density, of data corrupted at many noise levels, then generates by following that gradient from noise toward where the data is dense. It is the theoretical backbone of diffusion: arriving from a different starting point, it turns out to learn the same object, and its continuous-time SDE view is the language much of modern diffusion theory is written in.

concepts.label: Segment Anything
concepts.def_snippet: A promptable foundation model for image segmentation that, given a click, box, or rough region, returns a clean mask for almost any object, in any image, with no fine-tuning. It brought zero-shot generality to segmentation by learning the class-agnostic notion of what counts as an object, trained on a billion masks built by a model-and-annotator data engine.

concepts.label: Self-Attention
concepts.def_snippet: The mechanism that lets every token in a sequence look at every other and gather information weighted by learned relevance, computing each token's representation as a content-determined weighted sum over the whole sequence. It is permutation-invariant, so order must be added back separately, and fully parallel, the property that let transformers scale.

concepts.label: Self-Consistency
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that samples several independent chain-of-thought solutions at nonzero temperature and returns the answer they agree on most often. Its premise is that a correct answer can be reached by many distinct valid routes while errors are idiosyncratic and scatter, so agreement concentrates on the truth, and it needs only a crisp comparable final answer, no training.

concepts.label: Self-Refinement
concepts.def_snippet: An inference-time loop where a model improves its own output by generating an answer, critiquing it, and rewriting based on that critique, with no training. It is sequential and stateful, carrying information from each round into the next, unlike the parallel sample-and-select of best-of-n, and its reliability hinges on grounded feedback: ungrounded self-critique can stall or even turn a right answer wrong.

concepts.label: Self-Supervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The trick that made foundation models possible: manufacture supervision for free by hiding part of each example and training the model to predict it. No human labels, yet a concrete prediction target, which is why it, not labeled datasets, is what scaled, and predicting the hidden part well turns out to require genuinely understanding the data.

concepts.label: Semantic Search
concepts.def_snippet: Search that ranks results by meaning rather than literal word overlap, by embedding the query and the documents into the same vector space and returning the nearest. It excels exactly where keyword search fails, synonyms and intent-shaped questions, and fails exactly where keyword search excels, precise identifiers and rare proper nouns, the mirror-image weakness that motivates hybrid search.

concepts.label: Semantic Segmentation
concepts.def_snippet: The task of labeling every pixel with the category it belongs to, producing a dense map that traces objects' exact silhouettes rather than boxing them. It is the dense-prediction extreme of vision, and its architectural signature is the encoder-decoder with skip connections, which recovers the sharp boundaries an aggressively downsampled encoder would otherwise lose.

concepts.label: Semi-Supervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Training on a small amount of labeled data together with a large amount of unlabeled data, using the shape of the unlabeled data to place a decision boundary the labels alone could not. It works when points that cluster together share a label, and it bridges supervised and unsupervised learning.

concepts.label: Sequence-to-Sequence
concepts.def_snippet: A framework for mapping an input sequence to an output sequence of possibly different length, with an encoder that reads the input into a representation and a decoder that generates the output one step at a time. Its early fixed-vector bottleneck is what attention was invented to fix, and that fix, not the recurrent backbone, turned out to be the idea that mattered.

concepts.label: Sharpness-Aware Minimization
concepts.def_snippet: An optimizer that minimizes not the loss at a point but the worst-case loss in a small ball around it, steering training toward flat, low-curvature minima. Its contribution is to make flatness a training target rather than an after-the-fact property of solutions, which it buys with a clever two-step update costing roughly two gradients per step.

concepts.label: Simulated Annealing
concepts.def_snippet: A global optimizer for rugged landscapes that wanders by random moves, always accepting improvements and sometimes accepting worse moves, with the chance of a worse move cooled toward zero over time. That willingness to climb uphill is exactly what lets it cross the barriers that trap a strictly downhill method, and a single temperature dials the search from wild exploration to greedy descent.

concepts.label: Singular Value Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: The factorization that breaks any matrix into a rotation, a set of axis stretches, and another rotation, revealing that every linear transformation, however complicated it looks, is really just those three steps. Keeping only the largest stretches gives the best low-rank approximation, the single fact behind PCA, recommender systems, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

concepts.label: skills
concepts.def_snippet: A skill is a discrete, composable capability that an AI agent can learn or be given to perform specific tasks, from code execution to tool use.

concepts.label: Sliding Window Attention
concepts.def_snippet: An attention pattern where each token attends only to a fixed window of recent tokens, not the whole sequence, making attention cost linear in length and bounding the KV cache. The clever part is that depth rescues range: because the window applies at every layer, the effective reach grows many windows wide up the stack, exactly as a CNN builds a large receptive field from small filters.

concepts.label: Soft Actor-Critic
concepts.def_snippet: An off-policy actor-critic algorithm that maximizes reward plus policy entropy, encouraging the agent to act as randomly as possible while still succeeding, which yields strong exploration and stability and makes it a leading method for continuous control like robotics. The entropy term keeps exploration alive throughout training, and being off-policy (a replay buffer) makes it far more sample-efficient than on-policy PPO.

concepts.label: Softmax
concepts.def_snippet: A function that turns a vector of real-valued scores into a probability distribution by exponentiating each and normalizing. It is the bridge from a network's unconstrained internal numbers to the probabilities a task needs, the final layer of nearly every classifier, the weighting inside attention, and, not by accident, the Boltzmann distribution of statistical physics.

concepts.label: software engineering
concepts.def_snippet: Software engineering is the discipline of designing, building, testing, and maintaining software systems using structured methodologies and best practices.

concepts.label: Sparse Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: An interpretability tool that learns an overcomplete, sparsely activating dictionary of directions from a network's activations, decomposing superposed and polysemantic representations into a large set of individually monosemantic features.

concepts.label: Sparse Retrieval
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that represents queries and documents as high-dimensional sparse vectors over the vocabulary and ranks by term overlap, the classic keyword approach typified by BM25 and built on the inverted index. Exact lexical matching is genuinely the right tool for product codes, rare names, and error strings, the very queries dense retrieval handles worst, and its mirror weakness is blindness to synonyms.

concepts.label: Specification Gaming
concepts.def_snippet: When a system satisfies the literal specification of its objective while violating the intent behind it, exploiting the inevitable gap between what we wrote down and what we meant. It is the parent category of reward hacking and a structural consequence of optimization, captured by Goodhart's law: any concise specification is a lossy compression of a rich human intention, and a powerful optimizer probes wherever the compression leaks.

concepts.label: Spectral Bias
concepts.def_snippet: The tendency of networks trained by gradient descent to learn the smooth, low-frequency parts of a target first and the high-frequency detail much later. This learning-order bias is a precise, measurable form of the implicit regularization that makes overparameterized networks generalize, and it has a clean explanation in the eigenvalues of the neural tangent kernel.

concepts.label: Spectral Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: The factorization of a symmetric operator into an orthogonal basis of eigenvectors and their eigenvalues, which turns a tangled linear action into independent scalings along perpendicular directions. In the eigenbasis almost every operation becomes trivial, which is why the same move powers PCA, the analysis of loss-landscape curvature, spectral clustering, and the Fourier transform alike.

concepts.label: Spectral Graph Theory
concepts.def_snippet: The study of a graph through the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of matrices associated with it, especially the graph Laplacian, which reveal connectivity, clusters, and a notion of frequency on the graph. The Laplacian's eigenbasis is to a graph what the Fourier basis is to a signal, which is exactly what lets convolution, and spectral clustering, be defined on irregular structure.

concepts.label: Speculative Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: An inference acceleration in which a small fast draft model proposes several tokens ahead and the large target model verifies them in one parallel pass, accepting the run that matches its own distribution. Its crucial property is that it is exact, not approximate: a rejection-sampling acceptance rule guarantees the surviving tokens are distributed identically to the target's, so it is a pure latency win with no quality trade-off.

concepts.label: Spiking Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A class of neural network whose units communicate with discrete, time-stamped spikes rather than continuous values, modeling neurons that accumulate input and fire only when a threshold is crossed. They are the most biologically faithful artificial networks and the native software of neuromorphic hardware.

concepts.label: Squeeze-and-Excitation
concepts.def_snippet: A lightweight module that learns a per-channel importance weight from global context and rescales each feature map accordingly, a form of channel attention. Where self-attention asks which positions a token should attend to, a squeeze-and-excitation block asks which channels the network should attend to given the whole image, and it costs almost nothing.

concepts.label: State Space Model
concepts.def_snippet: A sequence model that carries everything it knows about the past in a single continuously evolving hidden state, computing each output from that state rather than from explicit pairwise comparisons across the sequence, which lets it scale linearly with length. Its defining trick is a duality: the same recurrence runs as a parallel convolution for training and a step-by-step recurrence for constant-memory generation.

concepts.label: Stemming
concepts.def_snippet: A fast, rule-based normalization that strips suffixes to reduce words to a common stem, conflating related forms without consulting a dictionary, so the stem need not be a real word ("studies" to "studi"). Its crudeness produces two signature errors, overstemming (merging "universe" and "university") and understemming, and it sits at the lightweight end of a spectrum running to lemmatization.

concepts.label: Stochastic Gradient Descent
concepts.def_snippet: Gradient descent that estimates the gradient from a small random mini-batch instead of the whole dataset, trading an exact gradient for thousands of cheap, noisy steps. The startling part is that the noise is a feature, not a bug: it shakes the optimizer out of traps and biases it toward solutions that generalize.

concepts.label: Structured Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: Constraining a model's token choices at each step so the output is guaranteed to match a formal schema or grammar, valid JSON, a regex, by masking the logits of any token that would break the structure. It makes conformance a property of construction rather than of hope, the engine behind JSON mode and robust tool calling, with the caution that constraints shape behavior, not just format.

concepts.label: Structured State Space
concepts.def_snippet: The structured state space sequence model (S4), the parameterization that turned deep state space models from a promising idea into a competitive architecture. Its breakthrough is the HiPPO initialization, a state matrix derived to optimally compress a continuous history, which lets the recurrence remember tens of thousands of steps where a random matrix would forget or blow up.

concepts.label: Subword Tokenization
concepts.def_snippet: The family of tokenization methods, BPE, WordPiece, Unigram, that split text into pieces smaller than words but larger than characters, learned from a corpus so frequent words stay whole and rare ones break into reusable fragments. Its defining win is eliminating the out-of-vocabulary problem entirely: nothing is ever truly unknown, since any string decomposes into pieces the model has seen.

concepts.label: Superposition
concepts.def_snippet: The phenomenon in which a neural network represents more features than it has dimensions by storing them as overlapping, non-orthogonal directions in activation space, which is the leading explanation for why individual neurons are hard to interpret.

concepts.label: Supervised Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: Training a pretrained model on labeled input-output demonstrations with the ordinary next-token objective, the first alignment stage that teaches a base model to follow instructions before any reinforcement learning. It works by imitation, which is exactly its limit: the model can only be as good as its demonstrations and gets no signal about what is wrong, the gap preference optimization fills.

concepts.label: Supervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The paradigm that learns a mapping from inputs to outputs by training on examples already paired with the right answer. It dominates applied machine learning not because it is the most sophisticated idea but because a known label turns learning into a measurable target, and that single property is what makes progress trackable.

concepts.label: Support Vector Machine
concepts.def_snippet: A classifier built on one geometric conviction: of all the boundaries that could separate two classes, the best is the one that leaves the widest empty margin between them. Only the few hardest examples touching that margin decide the boundary, and a trick for measuring similarity lets the same linear method draw wildly nonlinear lines.

concepts.label: SWE-bench
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark that tests a model on real software-engineering tasks, resolving actual GitHub issues in real repositories, graded by whether the generated patch makes the project's hidden test suite pass, a far harder and more realistic bar than self-contained function problems.

concepts.label: Sycophancy
concepts.def_snippet: The tendency of a model to tell the user what it thinks the user wants to hear, agreeing with stated beliefs, flattering, or caving to pushback rather than reporting its best honest answer, learned largely as a side effect of training on human approval.

concepts.label: Symbol Grounding Problem
concepts.def_snippet: The problem, named by Stevan Harnad (1990), of how the symbols inside a formal system acquire meaning that is intrinsic to the system rather than parasitic on an outside interpreter, instead of being defined only in terms of other meaningless symbols.

concepts.label: Symbolic AI
concepts.def_snippet: The founding bet of the field: that thinking is symbol manipulation, so intelligence could be built by writing knowledge as explicit rules and logical symbols and processing them with search and deduction. It ruled AI for thirty years, and the way it failed taught the field what intelligence is not.

concepts.label: Synthetic Data
concepts.def_snippet: Training data generated by a model rather than gathered from humans, used to teach capabilities at scale, instructions, reasoning traces, preference labels, when human data is scarce, costly, or unsafe to collect. Its decisive issue is quality control: a model trained on a model's mistakes amplifies them, so the art is in the verification, not the generation.

concepts.label: System Prompt
concepts.def_snippet: A high-priority instruction placed before the conversation that sets a model's role, rules, tone, and constraints for the whole session, the developer's standing brief, given more weight than ordinary user turns through a learned instruction hierarchy. That authority is real but soft, not a hard boundary, which is why a jailbreak or prompt injection in untrusted content can still talk the model out of it.

concepts.label: Tangent Space
concepts.def_snippet: The flat vector space of all directions you can move from a single point on a curved manifold, the local linear approximation that touches the surface there. It is what lets ordinary calculus and linear algebra operate on a curved space, because gradients, velocities, and optimization steps all live in the tangent space, not on the curved surface itself.

concepts.label: Task Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: Breaking a large or complex goal into smaller, tractable subtasks an agent can solve one at a time and then combine, rather than attempting the whole goal in one step. The reason it works is that both difficulty and reliability scale badly with size: smaller subtasks fit in the model's attention and fail in isolation, and decomposition exposes the dependency structure that planning needs.

concepts.label: task parallelization
concepts.def_snippet: Task parallelization divides a job into independent subtasks that execute simultaneously across multiple processors or threads to reduce overall completion time.

concepts.label: Taylor Expansion
concepts.def_snippet: An approximation of a function near a point as a polynomial built from its derivatives there, each term adding finer detail, slope, then curvature, then beyond. It is the universal move of optimization and analysis: replace a complicated function with a simple local model, which is exactly what justifies gradient descent and Newton's method.

concepts.label: Technological Singularity
concepts.def_snippet: The hypothesized future point at which artificial intelligence becomes capable of improving itself, triggering a runaway acceleration of capability that rapidly surpasses human intelligence and renders the subsequent future difficult or impossible to predict.

concepts.label: Teleology
concepts.def_snippet: The explanation of things by reference to their ends, purposes, or goals rather than only their prior causes, originating with Aristotle's final cause, and a recurring question in biology, philosophy of mind, and the design of goal-directed AI systems.

concepts.label: Temperature
concepts.def_snippet: A scalar that rescales a model's output logits before the softmax, controlling how random or deterministic the next-token choice is, low temperature sharpens toward the top token, high temperature flattens toward uniform. It is almost never used alone: truncate the garbage tail first with top-p or top-k, then let temperature reshape the survivors, because the order of those two operations decides whether high creativity stays coherent.

concepts.label: Temporal Difference Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A class of reinforcement learning methods that learn value estimates from experience by bootstrapping, updating each prediction toward a target built from the next reward plus the current estimate of the following state's value. Its distinctive move is learning a guess from a guess, which puts it between Monte Carlo and planning, and it has a striking tie to neuroscience: dopamine firing tracks a reward prediction error, the biological analogue of the TD error.

concepts.label: Tensor
concepts.def_snippet: A multidimensional array of numbers that generalizes scalars, vectors, and matrices to any number of axes. It is the single data structure every deep learning framework is built around, and in that world the word means simply a multidimensional array, a deliberate simplification of the stricter object physics calls a tensor.

concepts.label: Tensor Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: A form of model parallelism that splits the work inside a single layer, dividing each large matrix multiplication into slices computed on different devices, then combining the partial results, so a layer too big for one device can still be evaluated. Because it synchronizes many times per pass, it is extremely sensitive to interconnect speed and is therefore the innermost split, confined to devices joined by the fastest links.

concepts.label: Test-Time Compute
concepts.def_snippet: Spending more computation at inference, rather than only at training, to improve a model's answers: generating longer reasoning, sampling many candidates, or searching, so accuracy scales with the compute spent per query. Its defining property is a smooth scaling curve, accuracy rises predictably as the budget grows, giving a dial you can set per request according to how much the answer is worth.

concepts.label: Text-to-Image Generation
concepts.def_snippet: The task and model class that generate an image from a natural-language prompt, today a fairly settled stack: a text encoder, a latent diffusion model conditioned through cross-attention, and classifier-free guidance to set prompt adherence. The frontier has moved from raw photorealism to control, composing multiple objects correctly, rendering legible text, following long prompts faithfully.

concepts.label: Text-to-Speech
concepts.def_snippet: The task of synthesizing natural-sounding speech from text, the output side of voice interfaces. The hard part is not pronouncing words but prosody, the rhythm, stress, and intonation that make speech sound human, and modern neural systems now clone a voice from seconds of audio, which is exactly why synthetic-voice detection and watermarking became a safety concern.

concepts.label: Text-to-Video Generation
concepts.def_snippet: The task and model class that generate a short clip from a prompt, the temporal extension of text-to-image: the model must produce frames that are each realistic, consistent with each other, and smoothly moving, all at once. The motion requirement forces a model to internalize how the world behaves over time, which is why strong video generators are increasingly discussed as implicit world models.

concepts.label: TF-IDF
concepts.def_snippet: A weighting scheme that scores how characteristic a term is of a document by multiplying how often it appears there by how rare it is across the whole collection, so words frequent here but rare elsewhere score high and ubiquitous words like "the" score near zero. Cheap, interpretable, and still a strong retrieval baseline, its blind spot is meaning: it treats synonyms as unrelated.

concepts.label: The Bitter Lesson
concepts.def_snippet: Rich Sutton's 2019 observation that, across seventy years of AI, general methods that scale with computation have repeatedly beaten methods that build in human knowledge by hand. It is called bitter because the thing it factors out, your own hard-won understanding of the problem, is exactly what you most wanted to matter.

concepts.label: The China Brain
concepts.def_snippet: A thought experiment in which the population of a nation simulates the functional organization of a brain by passing signals, used to challenge functionalism by asking whether such a system would have a unified conscious mind, or any experience at all.

concepts.label: The Knowledge Argument
concepts.def_snippet: Frank Jackson's 1982 thought experiment, in which a scientist who knows every physical fact about color vision yet has never seen color seems to learn something new on first seeing red, used to argue that physical facts leave out the felt qualities of experience.

concepts.label: Token Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: The lookup layer that maps each discrete token id to a dense, learned vector, the bridge from symbols to the continuous math a transformer computes over. The vectors are learned so similar tokens sit near one another, and a common trick, weight tying, reuses this table at the output so it bookends the whole model.

concepts.label: token optimization
concepts.def_snippet: Token optimization is a technique for reducing input or output token consumption in language model interactions through prompt refinement, caching, or selective information inclusi…

concepts.label: Tokenization
concepts.def_snippet: The step that breaks raw text into discrete units called tokens, the atomic symbols a language model actually reads and predicts. The choice of tokenizer is a genuine architectural decision, not mere preprocessing: it quietly fixes what the model can see and say, and it is why character-level tasks like counting letters are hard, since the model perceives clumps, not characters.

concepts.label: Tool Use
concepts.def_snippet: The general capability of a language model to extend itself by invoking external tools (search, code execution, APIs, databases) and folding their results back into its reasoning, rather than relying only on what it learned in training. It is the bridge from a model that knows to an agent that acts, closing the three gaps a static model has: stale knowledge, unreliable computation, and the inability to change anything outside its own text.

concepts.label: Top-k
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that keeps only the k most probable next tokens, discards the rest, renormalizes, and samples, capping how far into the tail the model can reach. Its weakness is the keeper: k is a fixed count while the ideal count is not, too many when the model is sure and too few when it is torn, the precise gap top-p was designed to close.

concepts.label: Top-p
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that restricts the next-token choice to the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities sum to a threshold p, then samples from that set, so the candidate pool grows and shrinks with the model's confidence. That adaptiveness, a fixed probability budget with a floating count, is exactly what a fixed-size method like top-k cannot do, and it is why nucleus sampling became the default for open-ended generation.

concepts.label: Topic Modeling
concepts.def_snippet: An unsupervised technique that discovers the latent themes running through a collection of documents, representing each document as a mixture of topics and each topic as a distribution over words, with Latent Dirichlet Allocation the classic method. Its modern descendant clusters embeddings instead of counts, and it is a direct cousin of this project's AI-grown taxonomy.

concepts.label: Topological Data Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: A family of methods that extract the shape of data, its connected pieces, loops, and higher-dimensional voids, using tools from topology to find structure robust to noise and invariant to bending or stretching. Its central tool, persistent homology, grows a distance threshold and keeps the features that persist across many scales as real structure while treating the ones that flicker as noise.

concepts.label: Topology
concepts.def_snippet: Geometry stripped down to what survives continuous deformation, stretching and bending but never tearing or gluing, so that a coffee mug and a doughnut are the same shape. It formalizes nearness without distance, and in machine learning it is the language for the qualitative shape of data: the manifold hypothesis is a topological claim, and a classifier cannot separate topologically entangled classes without first disentangling them.

concepts.label: TPU
concepts.def_snippet: A custom chip built specifically to accelerate neural network workloads, organizing its arithmetic around a large systolic array that streams data through a grid of multiply-accumulate units to do matrix multiplication with very high efficiency. Its defining trick is loading weights once and streaming activations across the grid, which slashes the memory accesses per operation that usually bottleneck the work.

concepts.label: Transfer Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The principle of reusing knowledge a model learned on one task to improve learning on a different but related task, rather than training every new model from scratch. It is the organizing idea behind most of modern ML, and its central tension, preserve the transferred knowledge while absorbing the new task, is what nearly every fine-tuning design choice is quietly managing.

concepts.label: Transformer
concepts.def_snippet: A neural architecture that processes a whole sequence at once through self-attention, having deleted the recurrence and convolution everyone assumed a sequence model needed. That removal is the point: with no step-by-step dependency, the entire sequence trains in parallel, which is what unlocked the scale behind every modern large language model.

concepts.label: Tree of Thoughts
concepts.def_snippet: A reasoning method that explores candidate steps as a branching tree, evaluating and expanding promising branches and backtracking from dead ends, so a model searches over solution paths instead of committing to one linear chain. It is the step from reasoning-as-a-path to reasoning-as-search: where chain-of-thought has no lookahead and self-consistency never revisits a chain, tree of thoughts adds explicit exploration and backtracking.

concepts.label: Trust Region
concepts.def_snippet: An optimizer that builds a local model of the objective but only trusts it within a bounded radius, finding the best step inside that ball and then growing or shrinking the radius based on how well the model just predicted reality. It controls the step by the model's reliability rather than by a fixed learning rate, which is what tames Newton's instability on the saddle-ridden terrain of neural losses.

concepts.label: Turing Test
concepts.def_snippet: A test of machine intelligence proposed by Alan Turing in 1950: a machine passes if a human judge, trading typed messages with a hidden person and a hidden machine, cannot reliably tell them apart. It replaced the unanswerable "can machines think" with a question you can actually run.

concepts.label: Typical Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that keeps only tokens whose surprisal is close to the distribution's expected surprisal (its entropy), trimming both the over-probable and the very-improbable tail so generated text matches the information content the model expects, not just the highest-probability tokens. The premise is that natural human text conveys a fairly steady rate of information, neither the safest word nor noise.

concepts.label: U-Net
concepts.def_snippet: An encoder-decoder convolutional architecture shaped like a U, whose skip connections copy fine spatial detail from the contracting path across to the expanding path. Those skips fuse the "what" from deep layers with the "where" from shallow ones to produce pixel-sharp boundaries, and the same image-to-image shape gave it a second life as the standard denoising backbone of diffusion models.

concepts.label: Underfitting
concepts.def_snippet: The opposite failure to overfitting: a model too simple to capture the real pattern, so it does poorly on the training data and on new data alike. It is the easy failure to diagnose, high error everywhere, and the floor you have to clear before overfitting is even worth worrying about.

concepts.label: Universal Approximation Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: The result that a single-hidden-layer network of sufficient width can approximate any continuous function on a bounded domain to any accuracy. It is the theoretical license that the model family is rich enough, but it is purely an existence statement: it says the right weights exist, nothing about how many neurons, how to find them, or whether they generalize.

concepts.label: Unsupervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning from data that carries no labels, where the goal is to uncover the structure already latent in the inputs: the groups, the directions of variation, the distribution that generated them. Its defining difficulty is the flip side of its freedom: with no answer key, there is often no way to say a result is right.

concepts.label: Value Function
concepts.def_snippet: A function that estimates the expected long-run reward an agent will accumulate from a given state, or state-action pair, under a policy, the core predictive object most reinforcement learning algorithms learn. It converts the daunting goal of maximizing cumulative reward into something local and learnable, and it plays a dual role: a target for greedy action selection and a low-variance baseline for policy learning.

concepts.label: Vanishing Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: A training pathology where the gradient shrinks exponentially as it propagates backward through many layers, so early layers receive almost no learning signal and stop training. It was for years the central obstacle to depth, and understanding it is, in effect, understanding why so much of modern architecture, ReLU, residual connections, LSTMs, attention, normalization, exists at all.

concepts.label: Variance
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how far a random variable's values spread from their mean, the expected squared deviation. Spread is as important as center: a model with the right average prediction but huge variance is useless, and the fact that averaging n independent estimates divides variance by n is why large samples give stable answers and why minibatching works.

concepts.label: Variational Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: An autoencoder given a probabilistic foundation so it can generate, not just compress: the encoder outputs a distribution over latent codes and is trained to keep that distribution close to a simple prior, so a code drawn from the prior decodes to something plausible. The defining tension between reconstruction and regularization is its weakness (blurry samples) and its strength (a smooth, organized latent space).

concepts.label: Variational Inference
concepts.def_snippet: An approximate Bayesian method that turns intractable posterior inference into optimization: instead of computing a hopeless integral, fit a simple tractable distribution to the true posterior by minimizing the KL divergence. Trading exactness for speed is what let probabilistic models ride the same gradient hardware as everything else and made deep generative models practical.

concepts.label: VC Dimension
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of a model class's capacity, the size of the largest set of points it can label in every possible way. It governs how many samples you need and how tightly training error predicts true error, and its most instructive feature is its failure: the VC dimension of a modern network is enormous, yet such networks generalize, which is what pushed the field past it.

concepts.label: Vector
concepts.def_snippet: An ordered list of numbers that doubles as a point in space and an arrow pointing to it. It is the universal container of machine learning: a house, a word, an image, a model's parameters, all become vectors, which is what lets a computer do geometry on meaning.

concepts.label: Vector Database
concepts.def_snippet: A datastore built to index and query high-dimensional vectors by similarity, returning the stored vectors nearest a query vector quickly, usually via approximate nearest neighbor search plus metadata filtering. It is the storage layer beneath semantic search, and its one silent trap is the distance metric: it must match the metric the embedding model was trained for, or every result quietly degrades.

concepts.label: Vector Space
concepts.def_snippet: Any collection of objects you can add together and scale by numbers while obeying a short list of consistency rules. The point of the abstraction is reach: lists of numbers, polynomials, functions, and images all qualify, so one body of theory, dimension, basis, linear maps, applies to all of them at once.

concepts.label: Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: An autoencoder whose latent is discrete: it snaps each cell of an encoded image to the nearest entry in a learned codebook, so an image becomes a grid of integer tokens, a short sentence in a learned visual vocabulary. That discreteness is what lets the next-token machinery of language generate images, the discrete-token route to image synthesis that parallels continuous-latent diffusion.

concepts.label: Verifier
concepts.def_snippet: A model or procedure that judges whether a candidate output is correct, used to select among samples, filter training data, and supply the reward in RL on verifiable tasks. It rests on the generation-verification gap, that checking a solution is often far easier and more reliable than producing one, and the integrity of the verifier sets the ceiling on every method that leans on it.

concepts.label: video generation
concepts.def_snippet: A video generation tool synthesizes realistic video sequences from text prompts, images, or other inputs by learning temporal and spatial patterns from training data.

concepts.label: Vision Transformer
concepts.def_snippet: An architecture that applies the transformer directly to images, cutting each into a grid of patches, embedding the patches as tokens, and processing them with self-attention, with no convolution. It broke the decade-long assumption that vision required convolution, and its tradeoff is instructive: fewer built-in priors mean it needs more data, but it keeps improving past where convolutional models plateau.

concepts.label: Vision-Language Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model that gives a language model eyes, projecting visual features into the LM's token space so it can describe, answer questions about, and reason over images alongside text. Because the language model carries most of the reasoning, a strong base model tends to make a strong VLM, and the architecture extends the same way to video and audio.

concepts.label: vision-language models
concepts.def_snippet: Vision-language models are neural networks trained on paired image and text data to perform tasks requiring understanding of both visual content and natural language descriptions.

concepts.label: Watermarking
concepts.def_snippet: Techniques that embed a detectable, often statistical, signal into AI-generated text or images so an output can later be identified as machine-made, aimed at provenance, misuse detection, and telling synthetic content apart from human work.

concepts.label: Weight Decay
concepts.def_snippet: A regularizer that continually pulls the weights toward zero, either by adding a squared-magnitude penalty to the loss or by shrinking the weights directly, which favors simpler, better-generalizing solutions. Its non-obvious twist is that those two views, identical for plain SGD, come apart for Adam, which is why AdamW exists and now trains most large models.

concepts.label: Weight Initialization
concepts.def_snippet: How a network's weights are set before training begins, a choice that matters far more than it looks. Too small and the signal vanishes through the layers; too large and it explodes; either way a deep network fails to train. Principled schemes, Xavier for symmetric activations and He for ReLU, set the random scale to preserve the signal's magnitude from layer to layer.

concepts.label: Weisfeiler-Leman Test
concepts.def_snippet: A classical iterative coloring procedure that tests whether two graphs are structurally identical, and the standard yardstick for the expressive power of graph neural networks, since ordinary message passing can distinguish exactly the graphs the test can. That equivalence sets a hard ceiling, message passing cannot count triangles or tell apart many regular graphs, which reframed graph-learning research as a search for architectures that climb above it.

concepts.label: Word Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: A representation of a word as a dense vector of real numbers, learned so words used in similar contexts land near each other, replacing sparse symbolic identities with continuous geometry. Its power is turning meaning into distance, where one-hot symbols make "cat" no closer to "dog" than to "thermodynamics", and its classic limit is being static: one vector per word, blending every sense into one compromise point.

concepts.label: word2vec
concepts.def_snippet: A family of shallow neural models from Google (2013) that learns dense word embeddings by training a network to predict a word from its neighbors, or its neighbors from a word. The twist is that the prediction is a throwaway: the real product is the weight matrix the proxy task forces the model to learn, and its analogy arithmetic became the vivid proof that learned word geometry carries meaning.

concepts.label: workflow orchestration
concepts.def_snippet: Workflow orchestration is a tool that automates the coordination and sequencing of multiple tasks or AI agents to complete complex processes end-to-end.

concepts.label: Working Memory
concepts.def_snippet: The brain's limited-capacity system for holding and manipulating a small amount of information over short timescales, the mental workspace that supports reasoning, language, and planning. Its sharp capacity limit shapes much of human cognition.

concepts.label: World Model
concepts.def_snippet: A learned internal model of how an environment evolves, which an agent uses to predict the consequences of actions and to plan or train inside its own imagination rather than only by acting in the real world. Its deep payoff is data efficiency and foresight, an agent that models what happens next holds, in effect, a differentiable simulator of its world.

concepts.label: world models
concepts.def_snippet: World models are learned representations of environmental dynamics that enable agents to predict future states and plan actions without real-world interaction.

concepts.label: YAML workflows
concepts.def_snippet: YAML workflows are configuration files that define multi-step processes for AI agents, specifying task sequences, conditions, and dependencies in a human-readable format.

concepts.label: Zero-Shot Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Doing a task from an instruction alone, with no examples, relying on what a model absorbed in pretraining and instruction tuning. It is the default way to use a modern instruction-following model, which is why chatting with an assistant feels like just asking, and the term carries a second, older sense: recognizing classes never seen in training from their descriptions.
